articles.headline: ANNIVERSARY OF THE PENNSYLVANIA ANTI-SLAVERY SOCIETY — A Letter from the Editor
articles.author: Benjamin Lundy (editor)
articles.summary: The editor reports from Harrisburg on the Pennsylvania Anti-Slavery Society convention that opened Jan. 16, 1838, with about 100 delegates. Dr. F. J. LeMoyne of Washington Co. presided; Dr. Bartholomew Fussell of Philadelphia and Rev. N. Stem of Harrisburg were vice presidents; Benjamin S. Jones, Dr. Hamilton, and Daniel Neall Jr. were appointed secretaries.

articles.headline: Chester County Anti-Slavery Society — Meeting at Coatesville
articles.author: Thomas S. Cavender, Secretary
articles.summary: Minutes of the Chester County Anti-Slavery Society meeting at Coatesville Meeting-house (adjourned session Nov. 29). Adopted resolutions of the Massachusetts A.S. Society board on the Alton mob and the murder of Elijah P. Lovejoy, appropriated $50 to the county Anti-Slavery Society, pledged $500 to the State Society, and appointed delegates from each local auxiliary society to the state meeting at Harrisburg on Jan. 16, 1838. Also appointed committees on statistics of colored persons convicted of offences in Chester County and to address the colored people of the county on moral and intellectual improvement.

articles.headline: MEMORIALS TO CONGRESS
articles.summary: Pennsylvania Senator S. McKean presented anti-slavery memorials to Congress signed by 4,787 persons.

articles.headline: PENNSYLVANIA AND MARYLAND — Important Slave Case
articles.summary: Reports tension between Pennsylvania and Maryland over a kidnapping case: Pennsylvania's governor demanded delivery of persons charged with kidnapping alleged fugitives; discusses conflict between the 1780 gradual abolition act, Pennsylvania jurisdiction law, and Maryland slave law; a mother and children adjudicated free in Pennsylvania were removed and held in slavery in Maryland.

articles.headline: ELIZABETH HARDIN
articles.author: W.H. (Buckingham, 1st mo. 7, 1838)
articles.summary: Tribute to Elizabeth Hardin, a poor colored washerwoman of Philadelphia who, though living only on her daily labor and suffering from cancer, purchased her own freedom and then relieved three members of her family from bondage — most recently her late husband's son by a former marriage, paying about $110 toward his $150 price after he fled his Maryland master.

articles.headline: CALHOUN'S RESOLUTIONS
articles.summary: Five of Calhoun's resolutions passed the U.S. Senate; the sixth was laid on the table by a heavy majority.

articles.headline: DISCUSSIONAL MEETING (notice)
articles.author: Thomas S. Cavender, Sec.
articles.summary: Notice that a discussional meeting of the Junior Anti-Slavery Society of the City and County of Philadelphia will be held at the Hall of the Pennsylvania Library Company, Cherry Street below Fifth, Friday evening at 7 o'clock.

articles.headline: Died — Samuel Emlen
articles.summary: Death notice: Samuel Emlen, formerly of Philadelphia, later of Burlington, N.J., a valued member and elder in the Society of Friends, died in his 72nd year.

events.name: Chester County Anti-Slavery Society meeting
events.description: County society meeting; Lovejoy resolutions; delegates appointed to State Society.

events.name: Junior Anti-Slavery Society discussional meeting
events.location: Hall of the Pennsylvania Library Company, Cherry St. below Fifth, Philadelphia
